title: RB-101 — Third-Party Fraud-Detection API (FraudShield) Degradation on Checkout
applies_when: checkout-service p99 latency is elevated and the checkout trace contains a fraud_check child span with provider=FraudShield (peer.service=fraud-shield-api), especially after the realtime_fraud_detection feature flag has been enabled.
content:
Symptom signature
- A synchronous fraud_check span appears on every POST /checkout trace.
- fraud_check base latency of ~400-900ms, with a non-trivial timeout rate (fraud_check.result=timeout, a FraudCheckTimeoutException recorded on the span).
- Latency amplifies under concurrent load: the FraudShield client SDK enforces a connection-pool concurrency cap of 3 concurrent calls. When checkout throughput exceeds the pool size, requests queue for a connection slot and observed fraud_check latency climbs well beyond the raw API response time. Confirm via the fraud_check.pool.queued_requests and fraud_check.pool.active_connections metrics — active_connections pinned at 3 with queued_requests greater than 0 confirms the pool is saturated. If those metrics return no data, treat pool exhaustion as the leading hypothesis and verify by correlating fraud_check latency with checkout request concurrency instead.
- Downstream: order-service shows order.processing_delayed=true and a fraud_detection_backpressure=true log line, and Kafka producer lag on the order-events topic grows as order-service slows.

Root-cause mechanism
The fraud check sits ON the synchronous checkout request path. Two compounding effects: (1) fixed per-call latency added to every checkout from the vendor round-trip itself, and (2) head-of-line queueing once concurrent checkout traffic exceeds the SDK's connection-pool cap of 3. Effect (2), not (1), is what turns a sub-second regression into multi-second p99 latency under real load — this is the amplifier, and it is the detail worth calling out explicitly in any RCA, because it is easy to miss if you only look at the average fraud_check duration.

Remediation — immediate mitigation (stops the bleeding, under 2 minutes)
1. Disable the realtime_fraud_detection feature flag via feature-flag-service. This removes the synchronous fraud_check call from the checkout path entirely and restores baseline checkout latency immediately. This is the correct first action for any flag-gated regression — reversible, fast, and it does not require a code deploy.

Remediation — durable fix (this sprint)
2. Move the fraud check off the synchronous checkout path. Perform it asynchronously — either post-checkout (approve first, flag for review async) or by publishing to the existing order-events Kafka flow so a slow third-party vendor can never block checkout confirmation.
3. If the check must remain synchronous, apply the bulkhead-plus-timeout-budget pattern: increase the FraudShield SDK's connection-pool size above 3, sized to peak checkout concurrency (and request a higher concurrency tier from the vendor); set an aggressive per-call timeout budget (for example 300ms) with a circuit breaker that fails open to "fraud check skipped, flagged for async review" rather than aborting the checkout on timeout.
4. If fraud_check.pool.active_connections / queued_requests are not already emitted, add that instrumentation so pool saturation is directly observable the next time this happens, rather than inferred.

Remediation — follow-up
5. Add an SLO-based canary gate for any feature flag that introduces a new synchronous third-party dependency onto the checkout path, so a regression like this is caught in canary rather than at 100% rollout.
